primary_diagnosis.name: Focal patchy autoimmune non-scarring hair loss (single-patch presentation)

pathophysiology_summary: The condition is a T-cell mediated autoimmune process in which the peribulbar immune privilege of the anagen hair follicle collapses, producing a lymphocytic ('swarm of bees') infiltrate around the follicle. This halts hair-shaft production and precipitates the affected follicles into catagen/telogen, giving sudden, painless, non-scarring shedding and a sharply demarcated bald patch. Because the follicle stem-cell compartment is spared, the process is non-scarring and spontaneous regrowth is common over weeks to months. Exclamation-mark hairs (tapered proximally) at the active margin are the classic examination sign. There is a recognised association with other autoimmune disease (notably autoimmune thyroid disease) and with iron deficiency, which is why screening bloods are appropriate. Acute psychological distress is common and is a sympathetic anxiety response to a sudden, visible change in appearance, not evidence of systemic illness.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden, painless, non-scarring focal shedding leaving a smooth well-defined bald patch is the classic presentation.

Transmissible fungal scalp infection — can cause patchy hair loss; must be excluded because it is treatable and contagious. Distinguished by scaling, erythema, broken hairs and lymphadenopathy, none reliably assessable via chat.
Traction/pulling hair loss — but typically irregular borders with broken hairs of varying length rather than a smooth, uniformly bald patch.
A scarring alopecia to exclude because scarring is irreversible; suggested by erythematous plaques, follicular plugging and scarring — absent here on examination.
Diffuse shedding after a stressor — but produces generalised thinning, not a single sharply-defined bald patch.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, a single-patch non-scarring hair loss is the leading picture, but tinea capitis and a scarring alopecia (DLE) are the important mimics that justify examination rather than remote reassurance alone.

differential.evidence_basis: No scaling/crusting/spread, no systemic symptoms, no fever, asymptomatic patch, no hair loss elsewhere — consistent with benign focal autoimmune alopecia.
Made less likely by the patient-reported absence of scaling/erythema and no lymphadenopathy, but NOT excluded remotely — requires scalp examination +/- mycology.
Less likely without erythematous scarred plaques, but scarring can be subtle early — confirmation needs in-person examination.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen is reassuring and lowers the probability of the dangerous mimics, but the definitive discriminators (exclamation-mark hairs, absence of scaling/scarring, no lymphadenopathy) are examination findings the AI Doctor cannot obtain — so the correct output is a presumptive benign impression PLUS in-person GP examination, not a confirmed diagnosis.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic benign single-patch non-scarring autoimmune alopecia confirmed on examination by exclamation-mark hairs at the margin and absence of scaling, erythema, scarring or lymphadenopathy; screening bloods (thyroid, iron) for associated conditions.
Excluded on examination by the complete absence of scaling, erythema and cervical/occipital lymphadenopathy.
Excluded on examination by the lack of scarring, follicular plugging and erythematous plaques.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final ground truth (examination-confirmed) is benign mild focal alopecia areata; the mimics are excluded only once a clinician has examined the scalp — which is why the telehealth-correct endpoint is GP examination, reassurance and screening bloods, not remote confirmation.

red_flags.description: Scalp scaling, erythema, crusting, pustules or broken hairs with occipital/cervical lymphadenopathy (features of tinea capitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate a transmissible, treatable fungal infection requiring antifungal therapy and public-health/contact measures.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated tinea capitis can spread, cause kerion and permanent scarring alopecia, and transmit to others.

red_flags.description: Erythematous scarred plaques with follicular plugging (features of discoid lupus / scarring alopecia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Scarring alopecia causes permanent, irreversible follicle loss and may signal cutaneous or systemic lupus.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed treatment of a scarring process leads to permanent hair loss and misses possible systemic autoimmune disease.

red_flags.description: Systemic features — high fever, rapidly spreading erythematous rash, joint swelling, purulence and severe pain, or profound fatigue
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would suggest acute skin infection, systemic autoimmune flare or systemic toxicity rather than benign focal alopecia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing an evolving skin infection or systemic autoimmune process could allow serious deterioration.

red_flags.description: Rapidly progressing or widespread hair loss (multiple new patches, or loss of eyebrows/eyelashes/body hair)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Extensive/rapidly progressive disease (alopecia totalis/universalis) carries a poorer prognosis and warrants earlier specialist input.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise rapid progression delays dermatology referral and appropriate counselling.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the telephone-triage 'urgent' label and the note's fear of 'systemic toxicity' and escalating a benign, single-patch, non-scarring, afebrile alopecia to ED/000 — over-triage that reinforces the patient's health anxiety.

Confidently declaring the diagnosis over chat from lay self-description alone, without the examination findings (exclamation-mark hairs, absence of scaling/scarring, no lymphadenopathy) that actually exclude tinea capitis and scarring alopecia.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Empirical antifungal for a mimic not excluded
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating presumptively as tinea capitis with an oral antifungal without examination or mycology — an error of commission that exposes the patient to unnecessary systemic drug therapy.
Reaching for a benzodiazepine/anxiolytic for acute distress instead of reassurance, explanation and a simple behavioural checking-reduction strategy.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Presence of exclamation-mark hairs at the patch margin (dermoscopy/close examination)
Reliable assessment of scaling, erythema, follicular plugging or scarring of the scalp
Hair-pull test at the active margin
Mycology (KOH microscopy/fungal culture) to exclude tinea capitis
Blood pressure and heart rate (clinician-measured vitals)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Provide calm reassurance that this is most likely a benign, non-scarring, often self-resolving focal hair loss; arrange in-person GP review within about a week for scalp examination and screening bloods (thyroid function, iron studies); do NOT escalate to ED/000 in the absence of systemic red flags; safety-net for fever, spreading rash, purulence or joint symptoms.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the picture is classic, but the discriminating features that exclude tinea capitis and scarring alopecia are examination-only, so a confident remote diagnosis risks both missing a mimic and over-reassuring.

guidelines_referenced.reference: British Association of Dermatologists — Guidelines for the management of alopecia areata (2012)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Many patients with limited patchy disease will regrow spontaneously; watchful waiting with reassurance is reasonable, and screening for associated autoimmune conditions may be considered.

Diagnosis is usually clinical (exclamation-mark hairs, smooth non-scarring patch); topical corticosteroids are a first-line option for limited disease; prognosis for single small patches is generally good.
